content: lmodulo is a full-stack SvelteKit + Fastify application scaffold built for teams that move fast. It ships with agile project management, in-app messaging, calendar events, fine-grained role-based access control, an AI assistant, invoice and billing management (Folio), and a CRM (Nexus) — all wired up and ready to extend. The scaffold is intended to be cloned as the starting point for a new project.

lmodulo's technology stack:

| Layer | Technology | Version |
|---|---|---|
| Frontend | SvelteKit + Svelte 5 runes | SvelteKit 2 |
| UI | Tailwind CSS + DaisyUI | Tailwind v4 |
| API | Fastify | v5 |
| Database | MongoDB | v7 |
| Storage | MinIO (S3-compatible) | Latest |
| Auth | Session cookies via @fastify/session | — |
| Language | TypeScript | 5.x |
| Runtime | Docker + Docker Compose | — |
| AI | Ollama (local LLM) | — |

lmodulo includes these built-in modules: Agile (milestones, sprints, jobs, tasks in a hierarchy; Kanban board, Gantt timeline, burndown charts, dependency tracking), In-App Messaging (email-style threaded messaging with rich-text, reply chains, archiving, live unread badge), Roles & Permissions (six built-in roles from Owner to Customer with granular CRUD permissions enforced on API and UI), Calendar Events (shared and personal events, time-off blocking, email reminders, searchable event browser), Folio (invoice management, client billing, recurring invoices, subscription billing, Stripe payments, client portal), AI Assistant (Ollama-powered chat panel, no API keys required), File Storage (attachments on jobs and tasks, avatar uploads, brand logo, backed by MinIO).

lmodulo runs entirely inside Docker. You do not need Node.js, MongoDB, or MinIO installed locally — Docker Compose handles everything. Prerequisites: Docker Desktop version 4.x or later (on Windows, use the WSL 2 backend), Git (to clone the repository), and Ollama (optional, install on the host machine for AI assistant support — the app connects to it at http://host.docker.internal:11434).

Clone the repository and enter the directory:

```
git clone <repo-url> lmodulo
cd lmodulo
```

Copy the example environment file and fill in your values. The only required change for local development is SESSION_SECRET:

```
cp .env.example .env
```

Environment variables:

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| SESSION_SECRET | — | Required. A long random string used to sign session cookies. |
| MONGO_DB | appdb | MongoDB database name. |
| APP_NAME | Application | Shown in the browser tab title. |
| APP_URL | http://localhost:3000 | Public URL — used to build links in emails. |
| SMTP_HOST | — | SMTP server for email (password resets, reminders). |
| SMTP_USER | — | SMTP username / sender address. |
| SMTP_PASS | — | SMTP password. |
| STORAGE_PROVIDER | s3 | Set to `local` to store uploads on disk instead of MinIO. |
| OLLAMA_URL | http://host.docker.internal:11434 | URL of your local Ollama instance. |

The development configuration adds bind mounts so the frontend recompiles on file changes. The API does not hot-reload on Windows — rebuild it after server-side code changes.

```
# Start all services in development mode
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.dev.yml up

# Or start in the background
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.dev.yml up -d
```

Windows Note: Bind mounts on Windows do not propagate filesystem events reliably. Frontend changes may not trigger a hot reload — run `docker compose ... build web` after editing .svelte files.

After starting the stack, access these services:

| Service | URL |
|---|---|
| Frontend (SvelteKit) | http://localhost:3000 |
| API (Fastify) | http://localhost:4000 |
| MongoDB | mongodb://localhost:27017 |
| MinIO Console | http://localhost:9001 |

The database is seeded on first run with demo users spanning all roles:

| Username | Email | Password | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| jnicora | joenicora@me.com | password123 | owner |
| knicora | kylenicora@me.com | password123 | admin |
| alex | alex@example.com | password123 | lead |
| jordan | jordan@example.com | password123 | contributor |
| sam | sam@example.com | password123 | viewer |

To rebuild and restart individual services after code changes:

```
# Rebuild and restart the frontend
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.dev.yml build web
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.dev.yml up -d web

# Rebuild and restart the API
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.dev.yml build api
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.dev.yml up -d api
```

lmodulo is a monorepo with two separate applications — a SvelteKit frontend and a Fastify API — connected by Docker Compose. Services:

| Service | Port | Technology | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| web | 3000 | SvelteKit | Serves the UI, SSR, and proxy routes to the API |
| api | 4000 | Fastify | Business logic, authentication, data access |
| mongo | 27017 | MongoDB 7 | Primary data store for all application data |
| minio | 9000 / 9001 | MinIO | S3-compatible object storage for file uploads |
| minio-init | — | mc (MinIO client) | One-shot container that creates the default bucket on first run |

The browser communicates only with the SvelteKit frontend (:3000). Server-side route handlers in SvelteKit forward authenticated requests to the Fastify API (:4000) by forwarding the session cookie. The API never exposes itself directly to the browser in production.

Request flow:
- GET /dashboard → SvelteKit (+page.server.ts load) → fetch http://api:4000/agile/milestones (internal)
- POST /api/agile/jobs → SvelteKit (+server.ts proxy route) → fetch http://api:4000/agile/jobs (forwards session cookie)
- GET /uploads/logo.png → SvelteKit → MinIO (proxied)

Project directory structure:

frontend/ — SvelteKit application
  src/routes/ — Pages, layouts, and API proxy routes
    +layout.svelte — Authenticated app shell (sidebar + content)
    (public)/ — Login, register, forgot-password pages
    api/ — Server-side proxy routes → Fastify API
    agile/ — Agile tracker pages
    messages/ — Messaging pages
    calendar-events/ — Calendar event pages
    documentation/ — This documentation site
  src/lib/
    components/ — Shared UI components
    config/ — Nav, theme, logo config
    stores/ — Svelte state stores
    utils/ — Data types and helper functions
    permissions.ts — Client-side permission checker

api/ — Fastify application
  src/routes/ — HTTP route handlers
    auth/ — Authentication endpoints
    users/ — User management
    roles/ — Role management
    settings/ — Application settings
    messages/ — In-app messaging
    agile/ — Agile module endpoints
    calendar-events/ — Calendar event endpoints
  src/plugins/ — Fastify plugins (auth, MongoDB, seed)
  src/lib/ — Shared utility functions

docker-compose.yml — Production service definitions
docker-compose.dev.yml — Development overrides (bind mounts)

lmodulo uses MongoDB directly — there is no ORM or model layer. Route handlers in api/src/routes/ query the database using the native MongoDB driver. Shared query logic lives in api/src/lib/ as small helper functions imported by multiple route files.

MongoDB collections: users, roles, sessions, settings, messages, agile_milestones, agile_sprints, agile_jobs, agile_tasks, agile_comments, calendar_events

Uploads (avatars, brand logo, job and task attachments) go to MinIO by default. The storage provider is configurable via the STORAGE_PROVIDER environment variable. Set it to `local` to write files to disk instead — useful for development without MinIO. Files are served back through the SvelteKit frontend at /uploads/[...path], which proxies to the API's upload serving logic. This keeps MinIO off the public internet in production.

The chat assistant connects to an Ollama instance running on the host machine at http://host.docker.internal:11434. This address resolves to the Windows or macOS host from inside a Docker container. The frontend proxies chat messages through /api/chat — responses are streamed back using server-sent events. If Ollama is not running or the model is not available, the chat panel degrades gracefully — it stays hidden until an admin enables it in Settings.

lmodulo uses session-cookie authentication. When a user logs in, the server creates a server-side session and sets a `session` cookie in the browser. Sessions are managed by @fastify/session on the API. Session data is stored in MongoDB under the `sessions` collection. When the SvelteKit frontend makes a server-side request to the API, it forwards the raw session cookie so the API can authenticate it. There are no tokens or JWTs.

New users register at /register. Registration can be toggled on or off by an admin in Settings. The first user to register is automatically assigned the `admin` role. All subsequent users receive the `customer` role — an admin can promote them afterwards. Usernames and email addresses must be unique. Passwords are hashed with bcryptjs before storage.

Registration fields:

| Field | Required | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| username | Yes | Unique, used for login display |
| email | Yes | Unique, used for login and password reset |
| password | Yes | Minimum length enforced by the UI |
| firstName | No | Display name in the sidebar and messages |
| lastName | No | Combined with firstName for full name display |

Users log in at /login with their email and password. On success, the API creates a session, sets the `session` cookie, and the frontend redirects authenticated users to /dashboard. Customers are redirected to /. Already-authenticated users who visit /login or /register are bounced away by hooks.server.ts before the page even loads.

hooks.server.ts runs on every request and enforces three layers of access control: (1) Unauthenticated redirect — any path not in PUBLIC_PATHS sends the user to /login. (2) Customer restriction — users with the `customer` role are limited to a small set of paths (home, profile, logout, upcoming events). (3) Permission-based guards — specific routes like /user-management require an explicit permission (users.read); failure redirects to /403. Public paths include: /login, /register, /forgot-password, /reset-password, /upcoming-events, and all paths under /documentation/.

The forgot-password flow generates a time-limited reset token and emails a link to the user. When the user clicks the link, they land on /reset-password?token=... where they set a new password. Reset tokens are single-use and expire after a short window. Email delivery requires a configured SMTP server via the SMTP_* environment variables. In development without SMTP, the reset token is typically logged to the API console.

Authenticated users can update their profile at /profile. Editable fields: first name, last name, username (must remain unique), email (must remain unique), avatar color (background color for the initials avatar), avatar image (upload a photo; replaces the initials avatar). Avatar images are stored in MinIO and served at /uploads/avatars/[userId]. Users can remove their avatar to revert to the initials display.

Authentication API endpoints:

| Method | Path | Auth | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| GET | /auth/config | Public | Whether registration is open |
| POST | /auth/register | Public | Create a new account |
| POST | /auth/login | Public | Authenticate and start a session |
| POST | /auth/logout | Required | Destroy the current session |
| GET | /auth/me | Required | Get authenticated user, role, and permissions |
| PATCH | /auth/profile | Required | Update username, email, name, avatar color |
| POST | /auth/avatar | Required | Upload a profile picture |
| DELETE | /auth/avatar | Required | Remove profile picture |
| POST | /auth/forgot-password | Public | Request a password reset email |
| POST | /auth/reset-password | Public | Set a new password using a reset token |

Six roles are seeded on first run. They can be edited by an admin, and new roles can be created from the Roles management page.

- Owner: Unrestricted access to every resource. Intended for the founding account or platform administrator.
- Admin: Full access equivalent to owner. Can manage users, roles, settings, and all content. The first registered user receives this role.
- Lead: Can create, read, and update agile resources (milestones, sprints, jobs, tasks, comments) and calendar events. Cannot manage users or system settings.
- Contributor: Read access to agile resources. Can create and update tasks and comments. Cannot create milestones, sprints, or jobs.
- Viewer: Read-only access to agile content and calendar events. Can send and receive messages. No write access to any content.
- Customer: External user role. No access to agile, messaging, or admin features. Restricted to a minimal set of public-facing paths. New self-registered users default to this role.

Permissions are defined as resource + action pairs. Each role holds a list of permitted combinations. C = create, R = read, U = update, D = delete.

| Resource | Owner | Admin | Lead | Contributor | Viewer | Customer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| users | CRUD | CRUD | — | — | — | — |
| roles | CRUD | CRUD | — | — | — | — |
| settings | CRUD | CRUD | — | — | — | — |
| agile_milestones | CRUD | CRUD | CRU | R | R | — |
| agile_sprints | CRUD | CRUD | CRU | R | R | — |
| agile_jobs | CRUD | CRUD | CRU | R | R | — |
| agile_tasks | CRUD | CRUD | CRU | CRU | R | — |
| agile_comments | CRUD | CRUD | CRU | CRU | R | — |
| calendar_events | CRUD | CRUD | CRU | R | CR | — |
| messages | CRUD | CRUD | CRU | CRU | CRU | — |

The hasPermission(user, resource, action) helper from $lib/permissions gates UI controls so users only see actions they can perform. Example:

```svelte
{#if hasPermission(data.user, 'agile_milestones', 'create')}
  <button>New Milestone</button>
{/if}
```

This is a UI convenience — the API always enforces the same rules independently. Hiding a button does not prevent a determined user from calling the endpoint directly, which is why API-level checks are authoritative.

Admins can create new roles and adjust permissions from the Roles page (accessible via the profile menu). Changes take effect the next time affected users log in, since permissions are loaded at login. Existing sessions continue with the old permissions until they re-authenticate.

The Fastify API runs at http://localhost:4000 in development. All endpoints accept and return JSON. Authentication is session-cookie based — include the `session` cookie on every protected request. Conventions: all list endpoints support `limit` and `skip` query params for pagination; IDs are MongoDB ObjectId strings; dates are ISO 8601 strings (e.g. 2026-01-15); a 403 response means the user lacks the required permission; a 401 means no valid session.

Users API — requires users.read/create/update/delete permissions.

| Method | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| GET | /users | List all non-customer users |
| POST | /users | Admin creates a user. Body: username, email, password, firstName?, lastName?. Defaults to viewer role. |
| PATCH | /users/:id | Update user profile fields |
| DELETE | /users/:id | Remove user permanently |
| PATCH | /users/:id/role | Body: { roleId: string }. Assigns a new role |

Roles API — requires roles.read/create/update permissions.

| Method | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| GET | /roles | List all roles with their permissions arrays |
| POST | /roles | Create a new role. Body: label, permissions[] |
| GET | /roles/:id | Get a single role by ID |
| PATCH | /roles/:id | Update role label or permissions array |

Settings API:

| Method | Path | Auth | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| GET | /settings | settings.read | List all settings as key-value pairs |
| GET | /settings/brand | Public | Returns { brandName, brandLogo } — used in the nav before login |
| GET | /settings/:key | Required | Get a single setting by key |
| PATCH | /settings/:key | settings.update | Body: { value: any }. Update any setting |
| POST | /settings/logo | settings.update | Multipart upload. Saves brand logo and returns URL |

Messages API — all endpoints require authentication.

| Method | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| GET | /messages | Inbox: threads where user is a recipient, not deleted |
| GET | /messages/unread-count | Returns { count: number } for the unread badge |
| POST | /messages | Send a new message. Body: to[], subject, body (rich HTML) |
| GET | /messages/:threadId | Get all messages in a thread |
| POST | /messages/:threadId/reply | Reply to an existing thread |
| PATCH | /messages/:threadId/archive | Move thread to archive |
| DELETE | /messages/:threadId | Soft-delete (removes from inbox, not from DB) |

Agile API — /agile. Milestones, sprints, jobs, tasks, and comments each follow the same CRUD pattern.

Milestones (/agile/milestones): GET list (with status, priority, search, limit, skip), POST create (title, description, strategicGoal, priority, status, startDate, endDate), GET /:id, PATCH /:id, DELETE /:id (cascade deletes all children).

Sprints (/agile/sprints): GET list (milestoneId, status, limit, skip), POST create (auto-increments sprintNumber within milestone), GET /:id, PATCH /:id, DELETE /:id (cascade deletes jobs and tasks).

Jobs (/agile/jobs): GET list (sprintId, status, category, blocked, search), POST create (with optional dependency IDs, validates no circular deps), GET /:id (full with tasks, comments, attachments), PATCH /:id, DELETE /:id.

Tasks (/agile/tasks): GET list (jobId, assignedTo, status, priority), POST create (assignedTo required when In Progress; blockedReason required when Blocked), GET /:id, PATCH /:id, DELETE /:id.

Comments (/agile/comments): GET list (jobId), POST add (jobId, content HTML), PATCH /:id (author or admin only), DELETE /:id.

Calendar Events API — /calendar-events.

| Method | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| GET | /calendar-events/events | Query: startDate, endDate, eventType, search, limit, skip |
| POST | /calendar-events/events | Create event. Body: title, description, eventType, startDate, endDate, allDay, sharedWith[] |
| GET | /calendar-events/events/:id | Get event detail |
| PATCH | /calendar-events/events/:id | Update event fields |
| DELETE | /calendar-events/events/:id | Delete event |
| GET | /calendar-events/subscriptions | Get current user's reminder subscription settings |
| PATCH | /calendar-events/subscriptions | Update reminder preferences for event types |

The agile tracker organizes work into a four-level hierarchy. Each level rolls up completion percentages, effort hours, and counts from its children.

Milestone → strategic goal or product version (e.g. 'v1.1 Agile')
  └── Sprint → time-boxed iteration (e.g. 'Sprint 1 — Mar 1–14')
        └── Job → a deliverable unit of work (e.g. 'Design kanban board UI')
              └── Task → atomic work item assigned to a person

A milestone represents a major strategic goal or product release. It groups sprints and provides a top-level view of progress across a theme of work.

Milestone fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| title | string | Short name (e.g. 'v1.1 Agile') |
| description | string | Detailed description of the goal |
| strategicGoal | string | The business objective this milestone advances |
| priority | Low | Medium | High | Critical | Importance relative to other milestones |
| status | Planning | Active | On Hold | Completed | Cancelled | Current lifecycle state |
| startDate / endDate | date | Planned date range |
| completionPct | number (computed) | Rolled up from task completion across all child sprints |

Sprints are time-boxed iterations within a milestone. Each sprint has a capacity (in hours) reflecting how much work the team can commit to. Sprint numbers auto-increment within their parent milestone.

Sprint fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| milestoneId | string | Parent milestone |
| sprintNumber | number (auto) | Auto-incremented per milestone |
| title | string | Short descriptive name |
| capacity | number | Available team hours for this sprint |
| status | Planning | Active | Review | Completed | Cancelled | Sprint phase |
| velocity | number (computed) | Actual hours completed in this sprint |
| committedEffort | number (computed) | Total estimated hours across all jobs |

A job is a discrete deliverable within a sprint — the equivalent of a user story or feature ticket. Jobs can depend on other jobs, and the system validates that no circular dependencies exist. A job marked as blocked requires a reason.

Job fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| sprintId | string | Parent sprint |
| category | Feature | Bug | Tech Debt | Research | Type of work |
| status | Backlog | In Progress | Blocked | Review | Done | Cancelled | Current state |
| blocked | boolean | Quick flag for blocked status |
| dependencyIds | string[] | IDs of jobs that must complete before this one |
| estimatedHours | number (computed) | Sum of task estimate hours |
| actualHours | number (computed) | Sum of task actual hours logged |
| attachments | array | Uploaded files associated with this job |

Tasks are atomic work items assigned to individual team members. They track estimated, actual, and remaining hours, enabling accurate burndown calculations.

Task fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| jobId | string | Parent job |
| assignedTo | string (userId) | Required when status is 'In Progress' |
| status | Backlog | Ready | In Progress | Blocked | Review | Done | Task workflow state |
| priority | Low | Medium | High | Critical | Task urgency |
| estimateHours | number | Up-front effort estimate |
| actualHours | number | Hours logged against this task |
| remainingHours | number | Remaining effort — feeds the sprint burndown chart |
| blockedReason | string | Required when status is 'Blocked' |
| dueDate | date | Optional task deadline |

Agile module views:

- Kanban Board (/agile/board): Tasks organized into six columns by status (Backlog, Ready, In Progress, Blocked, Review, Done). Drag tasks between columns to update status. Filter by assignee or job.
- Timeline / Gantt (/agile/timeline): Jobs rendered as horizontal bars on a time axis, drawn with pure SVG. Visualizes overlapping work and schedule gaps.
- Sprint Detail (/agile/sprints/:id): Per-sprint view with a burndown chart, effort summary, job list, and task board.
- Job Detail (/agile/jobs/:id): Full job view with task list, dependency graph, comment feed, attachments panel, and effort bar.
- Agile Calendar (/agile/calendar): Renders sprint-linked calendar events in a monthly grid.

Status workflows per agile level:

| Level | Statuses |
|---|---|
| Milestone | Planning → Active → On Hold / Completed / Cancelled |
| Sprint | Planning → Active → Review → Completed / Cancelled |
| Job | Backlog → In Progress → Blocked → Review → Done / Cancelled |
| Task | Backlog → Ready → In Progress → Blocked → Review → Done |

Nexus is a lightweight CRM built for agile development shops. It tracks the full client lifecycle — from first contact through closed deal — and ties every interaction back to an activity log. The module lives at /crm. Data model hierarchy:

Company — an organization (prospect, customer, partner, vendor)
  └── Contact — an individual person linked to a company
  └── Deal — an active opportunity or engagement
        └── Activity — a logged interaction (call, email, meeting, demo, note, task)

Activities are polymorphic — linked to a company, contact, or deal via entityType + entityId.

A company is the top-level CRM entity. Each company carries a computed health score (0–100) derived from its open deal value and how recently an activity was logged.

Company fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| name | string | Company name (required) |
| domain | string (unique) | e.g. acme.com — sparse unique index |
| industry | SaaS | Enterprise | Startup | Agency | Government | Other | Sector classification |
| size | 1-10 | 11-50 | 51-200 | 200+ | Headcount band |
| type | Prospect | Customer | Partner | Vendor | Relationship type |
| assignedTo | userId | Team member responsible for this account |
| tags | string[] | Free-form labels |
| healthScore | 0–100 (computed) | dealScore (max 70) + recencyScore (max 30) |

Health score formula: dealScore = min(70, totalOpenDealValue / 10000); recencyScore = 30 if lastActivity ≤ 7 days, 20 if ≤ 30 days, 10 if ≤ 90 days, 0 otherwise.

Contacts are individual people. A contact can be linked to a company via companyId. Email addresses are globally unique (sparse index).

Contact fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| firstName / lastName | string | Required |
| email | string (unique) | Sparse unique — optional but globally unique when present |
| phone | string | Optional direct line |
| role | Decision Maker | Champion | Technical | Finance | Other | Buying-process role |
| status | Prospect | Active | Churned | Partner | Relationship lifecycle state |
| source | Inbound | Referral | Conference | Outreach | Other | How this contact was acquired |
| companyId | ObjectId ref | Their employer |
| assignedTo | userId | Account owner |

Deals represent active sales opportunities or engagements. They move through six stages. When a stage changes, the API automatically sets probability unless the client sends an explicit override. Moving to Closed Lost requires a lostReason.

Deal fields: title (required), companyId, contactIds[], stage (Discovery | Proposal | Negotiation | Contract | Closed Won | Closed Lost), value (deal value), probability (0–100, auto-set by stage), type (New Business | Upsell | Renewal | Partnership), expectedCloseDate, lostReason (required for Closed Lost).

Auto probability by stage: Discovery=10%, Proposal=30%, Negotiation=60%, Contract=85%, Closed Won=100%, Closed Lost=0%.

Activities are a unified log of all client interactions. They are linked to any entity (company, contact, or deal) via the polymorphic entityType + entityId pair.

Activity fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| type | Call | Email | Meeting | Demo | Note | Task | Activity category |
| title | string | Brief subject or description (required) |
| body | string | Freeform notes |
| entityType | contact | company | deal | Entity this activity is linked to |
| entityId | ObjectId ref | The specific record being referenced |
| scheduledAt | date | When the activity is planned |
| completedAt | date | Set via PATCH to mark complete |
| outcome | Answered | No-show | Left Voicemail | Productive | N/A | Result of the activity |
| assignedTo | userId | Who is responsible for completing it |

CRM views:

- Overview (/crm): Dashboard with KPI cards (open deals, pipeline value, closed-won revenue, activities this week), pipeline funnel chart, and recent activity feed.
- Pipeline Kanban (/crm/pipeline): Six stage columns. Drag deal cards between columns to change stage. Dropping into Closed Lost opens a modal to capture the lost reason. Column totals show aggregate deal value.
- Contacts & Companies lists (/crm/contacts and /crm/companies): Searchable, filterable lists with debounced search. Companies show a health score bar inline.
- Detail pages: Company, contact, and deal detail pages show inline editable fields, linked entities, and a scoped activity feed.
- Reports (/crm/reports): Four pure-SVG charts: pipeline funnel by stage, activity volume by type, monthly revenue trend (last 6 months), and summary KPI cards.

CRM API endpoints:

| Method | Endpoint | Permission | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| GET | /crm/contacts | crm_contacts:read | List with search, status, role, companyId filters |
| POST | /crm/contacts | crm_contacts:create | Create contact |
| GET | /crm/contacts/:id | crm_contacts:read | Single contact with companyName joined |
| PATCH | /crm/contacts/:id | crm_contacts:update | Partial update |
| DELETE | /crm/contacts/:id | crm_contacts:delete | Delete contact |
| GET | /crm/companies | crm_companies:read | List with healthScore computed; search, type, industry filters |
| POST | /crm/companies | crm_companies:create | Create company |
| GET | /crm/companies/:id | crm_companies:read | Single company with healthScore, dealCount |
| PATCH | /crm/companies/:id | crm_companies:update | Partial update |
| DELETE | /crm/companies/:id | crm_companies:delete | Delete company |
| GET | /crm/deals | crm_deals:read | List; stage, companyId, assignedTo, closingBefore, excludeLost filters |
| POST | /crm/deals | crm_deals:create | Create deal; auto-sets probability from stage |
| GET | /crm/deals/:id | crm_deals:read | Single deal with companyName joined |
| PATCH | /crm/deals/:id | crm_deals:update | Partial update; stage change auto-updates probability; lostReason required for Closed Lost |
| DELETE | /crm/deals/:id | crm_deals:delete | Delete deal |
| GET | /crm/activities | crm_activities:read | List; entityType+entityId, type, assignedTo, open filters |
| POST | /crm/activities | crm_activities:create | Create activity |
| PATCH | /crm/activities/:id | crm_activities:update | Update; send completedAt to mark complete |
| DELETE | /crm/activities/:id | crm_activities:delete | Delete activity |

Folio is lmodulo's finance module. Staff create and manage invoices, set up recurring billing schedules, and manage subscription plans that auto-generate invoices on a cycle. Customers access their invoices and pay through a dedicated client portal. The module lives at /folio for staff and /client-portal + /payments for customers.

Collections: finance_invoices (one-off, recurring template, or auto-generated invoice with embedded lineItems[]), finance_subscriptions (named billing plan that auto-generates draft invoices), finance_payments (one record per Stripe PaymentIntent attempt).

Invoice lifecycle statuses:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| draft | Created but not yet sent — invisible to the customer. Auto-generated invoices always start here. |
| sent | Staff clicked 'Send to client' — customer can now see and pay it |
| paid | Stripe webhook confirmed payment_intent.succeeded |
| overdue | Past dueDate and still unpaid |

Invoice numbers are auto-assigned in INV-0001, INV-0002, ... format. Moving from sent to paid is webhook-driven only.

Invoice fields: invoiceNumber (auto), customerId, companyId, lineItems[] ({description, quantity, unitPrice, amount}), subtotal/taxRate/taxAmount/total (computed), currency (ISO 4217), status, dueDate, notes, recurrence (optional), subscriptionId (optional), stripePaymentIntentId (optional), paidAt (optional), createdBy.

Any invoice can be turned into a template by enabling the 'Repeat invoice' toggle. The template document keeps its own recurrence sub-document; each time the scheduler fires and finds a due template, it clones the invoice as a new draft and advances nextDate.

Recurrence sub-document fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| enabled | boolean | Whether the scheduler should clone this invoice |
| frequency | weekly | biweekly | monthly | quarterly | yearly | Clone interval |
| nextDate | date | When the scheduler should next generate a copy |
| until | date? | Optional end date — recurrence disabled once nextDate exceeds this |
| dueDateOffsetDays | number? | Days after generation date to set dueDate on copies |
| generatedFromId | ObjectId? | Present only on generated copies — points back to the template |

Identifying template vs. copy: 'Recurring' badge = recurrence.enabled is true (template); 'Auto' badge = recurrence.generatedFromId is set (scheduler-generated copy) or subscriptionId is set (subscription-generated invoice).

Subscriptions are named billing plans stored in finance_subscriptions. Each active subscription generates a fresh draft invoice on every billing cycle. Unlike recurring invoice templates (which are clones of a specific invoice), subscriptions are a first-class entity — they can be paused, resumed, or cancelled independently.

Subscription lifecycle: active (scheduler generates invoices), paused (scheduler skips, no invoices generated), cancelled (permanently stopped; also auto-cancelled if nextBillingDate would exceed endDate).

Subscription fields: name, customerId, companyId?, lineItems[] (copied to each generated invoice), taxRate, currency, billingCycle (weekly | biweekly | monthly | quarterly | yearly), startDate, nextBillingDate (computed from startDate + billingCycle on create), endDate?, dueDateOffsetDays?, status (active | paused | cancelled), notes.

The scheduler is a Fastify plugin (api/src/plugins/scheduler.ts) registered after seed. It runs once immediately on onReady, then every 60 minutes via setInterval. You'll see 'scheduler tick' in API logs at startup.

processRecurringInvoices: Queries templates with recurrence.enabled=true and recurrence.nextDate <= now. For each template: clones the doc as a new draft, assigns a new invoiceNumber, sets recurrence.generatedFromId to the template's _id, then advances recurrence.nextDate. If the new nextDate exceeds 'until', sets recurrence.enabled=false.

processSubscriptions: Queries active subscriptions with nextBillingDate <= now. For each: generates a draft invoice with subscriptionId set, then advances nextBillingDate. If the new date exceeds endDate, sets status='cancelled'.

Smoke-testing: Set recurrence.nextDate or nextBillingDate to a past date directly in MongoDB, then restart the API — the onReady tick will fire immediately.

Users with the customer role land on the client portal after login and see only two routes:

- Client Portal (/client-portal): Compose new messages to staff using the rich-text editor; view all in-app threads the customer participates in.
- Payments (/payments): Lists all invoices scoped to the logged-in customer — including auto-generated ones. 'sent' and 'overdue' invoices show a Pay button; calls POST /finance/invoices/:id/pay to create a Stripe PaymentIntent, then mounts Stripe Elements. On success the invoice flips to 'paid' via the webhook.

On a CRM contact detail page, staff with crm_contacts:update see a 'Convert to Client' button. POST /crm/contacts/:id/convert-to-client performs three steps: (1) Creates a user with role: 'customer' and a 48-hour password-set token. (2) Sends a password-set email to the contact's address (Ethereal preview URL logged in dev). (3) Sends an in-app welcome message from the first owner/admin via the Messaging module.

Stripe environment variables:

| Variable | Service | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| STRIPE_SECRET_KEY | API | Server-side Stripe SDK (sk_test_... in dev) |
| STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET | API | Verifies Stripe-Signature on incoming webhook events |
| PUBLIC_STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY | Frontend | Initialises Stripe.js in the browser (pk_test_... in dev) |

The webhook endpoint POST /finance/stripe/webhook receives a raw body for HMAC verification. It handles payment_intent.succeeded — sets invoice status: 'paid' and inserts a finance_payments record. If STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is not set, POST /finance/invoices/:id/pay returns 503. Use test card 4242 4242 4242 4242 with any future expiry and CVC once configured.

Folio API endpoints:

| Method | Endpoint | Permission | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| GET | /finance/invoices | requireAuth | List invoices; customers auto-scoped. Query: status, subscriptionId, limit, skip, sort, sortDir |
| POST | /finance/invoices | finance_invoices:create | Create invoice; optional recurrence body field makes it a recurring template |
| GET | /finance/invoices/:id | requireAuth | Single invoice; scope-checked for customers |
| PATCH | /finance/invoices/:id | finance_invoices:update | Partial update; accepts recurrence field |
| DELETE | /finance/invoices/:id | finance_invoices:delete | Delete invoice |
| POST | /finance/invoices/:id/pay | requireAuth | Creates Stripe PaymentIntent; returns { clientSecret: '...' } |
| POST | /finance/stripe/webhook | Stripe-Signature | Raw body; verifies HMAC; updates invoice + inserts payment record on success |
| GET | /finance/subscriptions | finance_subscriptions:read | List subscriptions; customers auto-scoped |
| POST | /finance/subscriptions | finance_subscriptions:create | Create subscription |
| GET | /finance/subscriptions/:id | finance_subscriptions:read | Single subscription |
| PATCH | /finance/subscriptions/:id | finance_subscriptions:update | Partial update; send { status: 'paused' } to pause, 'active' to resume, 'cancelled' to cancel |
| DELETE | /finance/subscriptions/:id | finance_subscriptions:delete | Delete subscription (does not delete generated invoices) |
| GET | /finance/customers | users:read | List users with role: 'customer' with company name joined |
| POST | /crm/contacts/:id/convert-to-client | crm_contacts:update | Converts CRM contact to customer |
| GET | /health | Public | Returns 200 OK when API is reachable |

lmodulo includes a full email-style messaging system for in-app communication. Concepts: Thread — a conversation started by a single message; replies extend the thread rather than creating a new one. Recipient — one or more users addressed in the original message; all recipients see the thread in their inbox. Unread — each user has their own read/unread state per thread; opening a thread marks it as read. Archive — threads can be archived to move them out of the inbox without deleting them. Soft delete — deleting a thread hides it for the deleting user but preserves it for other recipients.

Navigate to /messages/compose or press the compose button in the messages sidebar. Required fields: to (one or more recipients selected from the user list), subject (short summary shown in the thread list), body (rich text content — supports bold, italic, lists, links via Tiptap editor). The editor outputs HTML stored in MongoDB and rendered safely in the thread detail view.

Messaging tabs: Inbox — threads where you are a recipient, not archived or deleted; Sent — threads you originated, ordered by most recent activity; Archive — threads you have archived (can be unarchived to restore to inbox).

The sidebar navigation shows a live unread count badge on the Messages link. The count is fetched from GET /api/messages/unread-count on every route change. Opening a thread clears the unread flag for that thread immediately. The unread count is also fetched on the server during the root layout load, so the correct number appears even on first page render without a client-side fetch.
All roles except customer can send and receive messages. Customers have no access to the messaging system. There are no further sub-permissions — any staff user can message any other staff user.

Calendar events carry a type that controls how they appear and what reminders are generated:

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| upcoming_event | General team events — all-hands meetings, demos, milestones |
| standup | Daily or recurring standups |
| sprint_planning | Sprint kickoff ceremonies |
| sprint_review | End-of-sprint demos and retrospectives |
| deadline | Hard deadlines tied to deliverables |
| personal | Individual events not shared with the team |
| time_off | Vacation or absence blocks — visible to the team |
| one_on_one | 1:1 meetings between two people |

Calendar views:

- My Calendar (/my-calendar): A grid calendar showing events you own or are shared with. Requires calendar_events.read permission.
- Public Events (/calendar-events): A searchable list of all team events, visible to all authenticated users. Upcoming events are also visible to unauthenticated visitors at /upcoming-events.
- Manage Events (/calendar-events/admin): Admin view for creating, editing, and deleting any team event. Requires calendar_events.create.

Calendar event fields:

| Field | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| title | Yes | Short event name shown in the calendar |
| description | No | Longer event details and notes |
| eventType | Yes | One of the event type values |
| startDate | Yes | ISO date string |
| endDate | No | Defaults to startDate for single-day events |
| allDay | No | Boolean — hides time display in the calendar |
| sharedWith | No | Array of user IDs who can see the event in their personal calendar |
| tags | No | Free-form tags for search and filtering |

Users can subscribe to email reminders for specific event types. Subscription preferences are stored per-user and can be updated from the Notifications settings page. A background job on the API sends reminder emails at the configured lead time before each event. Email delivery requires the SMTP_* environment variables to be configured. Endpoints: GET /calendar-events/subscriptions (fetch your current reminder settings for each event type), PATCH /calendar-events/subscriptions (update which event types trigger reminders and how far in advance).

Milestones, sprints, and jobs can each link to calendar events via a calendarEventIds array. This lets you attach sprint planning and sprint review ceremonies to the sprint record, and connect deadline events to job deliverables. The Agile Calendar view at /agile/calendar renders all sprint-linked events in a monthly grid.

Calendar permissions:

| Action | Required Permission | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| View public events | Public | Unauthenticated users can view /upcoming-events |
| View events in My Calendar | calendar_events.read | All staff roles have this permission |
| Create personal events | calendar_events.create | Lead and above |
| Create and manage team events | calendar_events.create | Leads, admins, and owners |
| Delete events | calendar_events.delete | Admin and owner only |

User Management is accessible at /user-management to users with the users.read permission. Shows all non-customer users in a table. Admins can: Create users (add team members directly without requiring self-registration; new users default to the viewer role), Edit profiles (update any user's name, username, or email), Change roles (promote or demote users by assigning a different role; role changes take effect on next login), Delete users (permanently removes user and session; content they created remains in the database).

Note: Permissions are loaded at login time. A user whose role is changed mid-session will continue with their old permissions until they log out and back in.

Roles Management is accessible from the profile dropdown to users with roles.read. Admins can: Create new roles (define custom roles with any combination of resource/action permissions), Edit permissions (add or remove CRUD actions on any resource for an existing role), Rename roles (change the display label without affecting users assigned to that role). The six default roles (owner, admin, lead, contributor, viewer, customer) can be edited but not deleted.

Settings are accessible at /settings to users with settings.read. All settings are stored as key-value pairs in MongoDB and editable through the UI without restarting the application.

| Setting Key | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| brand.name | — | Team or product name shown in the nav header |
| brand.logo | — | URL of the uploaded logo image |
| app.registration_open | true | Whether new users can self-register at /register |
| chat.enabled | true | Show or hide the AI assistant chat panel |
| theme.mode | light | Default theme; users can override individually |

The brand name and logo appear in the sidebar header. Both are runtime-configurable from the Settings page — no deployment required to update them. Brand name: plain text, set via PATCH /settings/brand.name. Logo image: upload PNG or SVG via POST /settings/logo (stored in MinIO, served through /uploads/). If both a name and logo are set, the logo takes precedence. Remove the logo by PATCHing brand.logo with an empty string.

Each user can configure which calendar event types trigger email reminders and how far in advance those reminders are sent. Settings are per-user and stored in the calendar_events.subscriptions collection. The notification settings page at /notifications/settings shows a toggle for each event type alongside a lead-time selector.

Toggle app.registration_open to false to close public registration. The /register page will render a 'Registration is closed' message. Admins can still create users directly via the User Management page regardless of this setting.

The chat assistant panel is shown to all authenticated users when chat.enabled is true. Disable it by toggling the setting in the Settings page — the panel disappears immediately on the next page navigation without requiring a redeploy. The assistant connects to Ollama at the URL defined by the OLLAMA_URL environment variable. If Ollama is not reachable, chat requests will time out gracefully.
